confirmations.reason: Project-Level AGENTS.md is exactly a repository-scoped instruction file that gives the agent standing instructions and project-specific context, directly matching the capability.
Plugins explicitly bundle skills, app integrations, and MCP servers into reusable workflows for Codex, directly matching the concept of packaged reusable extensions the agent can load.
The Hooks feature is described as injecting user scripts into Codex's agentic loop on lifecycle events (e.g., SessionStart), which directly matches running custom logic on agent lifecycle events.

AGENTS.md instruction files provide persistent custom guidance but are instruction/context files, not mechanisms to shape the system prompt, persona, or output style directly. The capability calls for system-prompt or output style customization; AGENTS.md is closer to the project-instruction-file capability.
Per-Agent Model Selection directly allows choosing which model each agent runs on, either auto-selected or pinned, which is exactly the capability described.
MCP (Model Context Protocol) is described as the standard way to connect Codex to external tools and context providers, directly realizing the external tool/connector protocol capability.
MCP Server CLI Management provides a CLI command to add and manage MCP servers, directly realizing the install, configure, and manage MCP servers capability.
OAuth MCP Login directly authenticates to MCP servers via OAuth, which is exactly the MCP authentication capability described.

MCP Tool Exposure describes servers exposing callable tools to the model — that is about surface/availability, not governance or allow-listing of which tools the agent is permitted to use. Allow-listing/governance is a control capability; exposure is the opposite direction.
The Compaction feature explicitly manages context window limits in long-running conversations by compacting accumulated history, directly realizing the context compaction capability.
Container Caching caches configured containers to accelerate task startup — this is infrastructure/environment caching, not prompt or context caching to cut LLM latency and cost.
Subagent Workflows describes Codex spawning specialized agents in parallel and collecting their results, directly matching the capability of spawning focused sub-agents to handle parts of a task.

Multi-Agent Workflows describes orchestrating multiple Codex CLI instances via the Agents SDK, enabling coordination and work-sharing among agents, directly realizing the multi-agent teams capability.

Automations describes recurring background tasks, not user-defined repeatable named multi-step task sequences (workflows/pipelines). Recurring background runs are closer to the scheduled-runs capability.
Git Worktree Isolation runs each automation in an isolated worktree, directly enabling parallel work on multiple branches without interference, matching the capability.
Cloud Environments provides managed cloud environments with installation control, caching, secrets, and defaults, directly realizing the cloud/container execution environment capability.
Automations runs tasks unattended in the background and reports findings asynchronously, directly realizing background/async execution.
Standalone Automations are described as independent scheduled tasks that run on their own, directly matching the scheduled/triggered runs capability.
The Sandbox feature is explicitly a constrained environment that lets Codex run local commands autonomously without unrestricted access, directly realizing sandboxed execution.
Approval Policy controls when Codex must stop and ask before crossing sandbox boundaries, directly realizing tool permission controls (approve/deny what the agent is allowed to do).
Network Access Control enables or disables outbound network access with optional domain allow-lists, directly realizing the network/egress control capability.

DNS Rebinding Protection defends against a specific network-layer attack within the sandbox, not against malicious instructions injected via tool output or web content into the agent's prompt. These are adjacent security concerns but not the same capability.

Codex Security is described as a security-review plugin and cloud service that scans repositories for vulnerabilities and validates findings, directly realizing the security scanning/threat model capability.

Custom Setup Script runs a user-defined script for complex environment configuration — this is a setup/init mechanism, not a dev-container integration (i.e., running the agent inside a defined devcontainer spec/runtime like .devcontainer).

Built-in Run Tracing emits structured records of model calls, tool calls, handoffs, guardrails, and spans — this is observability/tracing (likely from the OpenAI Agents SDK), not Codex-specific audit logging for compliance review of agent actions. The feature description does not match Codex's audit logging.
AI Code Review describes Codex reviewing GitHub PR diffs and posting standard GitHub code reviews focused on serious issues, directly realizing the automated code review capability.
Evals API allows configuring and running evaluations of model outputs programmatically, including against external benchmarks, directly realizing the evals/testing harness capability.

Multi-Device Session Handoff lets users resume an active Codex session from any signed-in device, directly realizing the resume/fork session capability (resume aspect confirmed; fork is not mentioned but resume is sufficient).
Remote Connections lets users use Codex from another device or machine to control an agent remotely, including starting/continuing sessions, directly realizing the remote control capability.
Task Completion Notifications explicitly deliver alerts when Codex finishes a task or requires user input, directly realizing the notifications capability.
Codex for Linear is a connector that lets users delegate work to Codex from Linear issues (a project/issue management chat-adjacent tool), directly realizing the chat-surface integration capability.

GitHub Integration is one specific integration surface (PR review + async cloud tasks via @codex mention), not a general enumeration or framework of access surfaces (terminal, IDE, web, cloud, mobile). It is a single surface, not the capability of defining/managing where the agent can be used.

The build-test-run loop describes the agent autonomously iterating through build, test, and run cycles — this is goal-directed autonomous iteration where the agent loops until a verifiable condition (passing tests/build) is met.
